preamble: The shift from the voiced velar stop [g] to the voiceless velar fricative [x] involves two phonetic processes: spirantization (weakening from a stop to a fricative) and devoicing. This shift is a defining characteristic of West Germanic languages, particularly Dutch and dialects of German.

examples.note: Begedkefat: post-vocalic /g/ → [ɣ] (voiced velar fricative) in Biblical Hebrew. Note: atlas maps g→x as the closest equivalent; Semitic reflex is voiced [ɣ].
Word-final 'g' is consistently spirantized and devoiced in standard Dutch.
Initial 'g' is also spirantized, often devoicing in Northern dialects.
Colloquial and regional spirantization of final velar stops.

Sogdian spirantized inherited /g/ to [ɣ] (voiced velar fricative) in all positions — even word-initially — unlike Middle Persian which preserved /g/ initially. This is the Eastern Iranian spirantization pattern. The atlas maps this as g→x (voiceless), but the Sogdian reflex is the voiced [ɣ]; devoicing to [x] occurred in final position in several Iranian varieties.

examples.from: PIE *k before consonant (yielding *g in voiced clusters)
examples.note: Proto-Iranian spirantization of velar stops in consonant clusters: *g-clusters → *ɣ/x. Cf. Avestan xšāθra- 'realm' and xvara- forms where cluster-initial velars surface as fricatives. The process parallels Germanic consonant shift but applies specifically to pre-consonantal environments.

sources: Windfuhr, G. (2009). The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
